guide_lines.text: Look for movement in the grass. The smallest twitch is usually something alive.
Lift a leaf, gently, and look underneath. A lot of the forest lives on the shaded side.
The pond edge gets busiest near sunset. Sit on the bank and wait a minute before you decide it is empty.
Birds notice you before you notice them. Stand still and they will come back.
Mushrooms and worms come up after rain. The soil is loud the day after a storm.
Cracks in the sidewalk are tiny meadows. A lot of city life starts there.
Look up once in a while. The sky changes more than the ground does.
Every find is a real observation. There is no rush, and there is no wrong answer.
If you cannot tell what it is, take a clear photo. You can figure it out later.
Try standing still for one quiet minute. The place starts to fill back in around you.
Look close, then look far. Most places are layered, and each layer has different neighbors.
A parking lot, a planter, a hallway window: anywhere can be a place to notice something living.
